facts_disclosed: Pivoted to commercial — hotels and corporate offices

turns.content: I have made a significant strategic decision for the firm and I need to think it through out loud, if you will indulge me.
Of course — thinking out loud is one of the best ways to stress-test a decision. What's the strategic shift you're working through?

We have pivoted toward commercial work. Hotels and corporate headquarters, primarily. Two hotel projects came in nearly simultaneously, and then a corporate headquarters shortly after. I said yes to all three, and that yes has changed the trajectory of Al-Rashidi Interiors in ways I am still processing.

That's a major move — commercial work operates on a fundamentally different scale from residential in terms of scope, timelines, budgets, and stakeholder dynamics. Three projects at once is a serious commitment. What drew you to say yes to all three rather than testing the waters with one?

Because opportunities of this calibre do not arrive politely, one at a time. They arrive in a wave, and you either ride it or you watch it pass. The hotel projects are boutique properties — one in Jeddah, one here in Riyadh. The corporate headquarters is for a financial services firm that wants their space to reflect a certain sophistication. These are not generic contracts. Each one has genuine design ambition behind it.

That's a good instinct — and the fact that all three have real design ambition matters enormously. There's a difference between commercial work that lets you do meaningful design and work that reduces you to a spec sheet. How is the scale shift feeling practically? Commercial projects require different coordination — contractor teams, procurement at volume, longer approval chains. Is your team structured for that?

We are restructuring as we go. I have brought on a project manager with commercial experience — someone who understands procurement timelines and contractor coordination at this scale. My design team is strong, but they are accustomed to the intimate feedback loop of working with individual clients. With a hotel, you are presenting to committees. It is frustrating when six people must approve a tile selection, but there is something genuinely stimulating about designing for a collective experience. A hotel lobby must create a mood that is welcoming but not generic, luxurious but not intimidating.

That's a fascinating reframe — moving from 'this space should feel like you' to 'this space should feel like a place.' The design intelligence required is arguably higher because a hotel lobby has to generate its own identity from pure design decisions rather than leaning on a client's personality. Are you approaching the two hotel projects differently from each other?

Completely differently. The Jeddah property is coastal — deep teals, warm sand tones, coral textures, as though the Red Sea has entered the building in an abstracted way. The Riyadh hotel is urban and mineral — warm concrete, burnished brass, desert stone. Each must feel rooted in its geography. I refuse to create interchangeable spaces. And the corporate project is perhaps the most intellectually challenging. A financial services firm that wants to project stability and precision without stiffness. I am working with charcoal, warm grey, touches of deep navy, and letting the material quality speak — fine stone, brushed metals, leather that ages beautifully.

Place-specific design is harder because you can't template it, but it's what makes a hotel memorable rather than just comfortable. And that corporate brief — feeling the investment without seeing it announced — is the hardest balance in commercial interiors. Financial firms often default to either aggressive power signaling or sterile modernism, and finding the middle ground requires genuine restraint. How is the revenue side of this shift looking?

The revenue potential is transformational. I will say that plainly. A single hotel project represents more revenue than several residential commissions combined. And the corporate work carries strong margins because the budgets are structured differently — there is a clarity to corporate procurement that residential clients rarely possess. If we execute these three well, Al-Rashidi Interiors will be operating at a level I have been working toward for years.

When a strategic shift aligns both the creative ambition and the financial model, you know you're moving in the right direction. Commercial work at this scale also generates referrals within the same ecosystem — developers and corporate clients talk to each other. How are you managing your own bandwidth through three simultaneous major projects?

I am learning to delegate in ways I never had to before. I cannot touch every material sample on every project — the scale simply does not permit it. But I can set the design direction, establish the palette, and trust my team to execute within that framework. It is difficult for me. I am a person who wants her hand on everything. But this is how firms grow. You move from making every decision to making the decisions that matter most.

That's one of the hardest transitions for any founder — moving from craftsperson to creative director. The instinct to control every detail is what made your work excellent in the first place, but it becomes the bottleneck at scale. Setting the direction and palette while trusting execution is exactly the right model. Your team learns and grows, you maintain creative coherence, and the firm can take on work that matches the full scope of your ambition. It sounds like this pivot is precisely where you need to be.
